Here are its main characteristics based on the sources:
It’s designed for offline-first applications, caching large amounts of data, and storing structured records or large files (blobs).
, it operates asynchronously, meaning it won’t freeze the user interface during read or write operations.
All data operations must happen within a “transaction.” This ensures data integrity—either the entire group of operations succeeds together, or they all fail safely.
It stores data in “object stores” (similar to tables in traditional databases) and allows you to query data using unique keys or specific indexes.
The tradeoff for this power is complexity. The native API relies heavily on events and requires managing schema versions manually.

The mental model is this: an IndexedDB database contains one or more
; each object store holds records; each record has a
on fields you want to search by frequently. Reads and writes happen inside
. Records in an object store are sorted by key, which is why lookups and ordered retrieval are efficient. (

Its major uses are practical and blunt: offline-first web apps, local caching of API data, storing large structured datasets in the browser, queueing work while offline and syncing later, saving user-generated content such as drafts, and storing binary data like files or blobs. MDN explicitly notes that IndexedDB is suited for significant amounts of structured data, including files/blobs, and is a better fit than Web Storage when the data is larger or more structured. (
The key advantage is not elegance. IndexedDB is not elegant. It is powerful. If

is a sticky note, IndexedDB is a filing cabinet with an index and a lock on the drawer. The cost is complexity: the native API is verbose, event-driven, and easy to misuse. That is why many developers wrap it with libraries like Dexie. But the underlying database matters because the wrappers are standing on its shoulders, not replacing its rules. This complexity follows from its asynchronous request/transaction model documented in the platform APIs. (

IndexedDB stores values using the browser’s

. That means it can store much more than plain strings. MDN notes that IndexedDB indexes can contain JavaScript data types and stored objects are serialized with structured clone semantics. In practice, that means you can store ordinary objects, arrays, dates, numbers, strings, booleans, blobs, files, and other cloneable structured data. (
A few practical categories matter most:
: strings, numbers, booleans, big integers in modern environments.
: hierarchical objects are explicitly part of the spec’s model. (

, the story is narrower. Keys are not “anything JavaScript can dream up at 2 a.m.” They have to be valid IndexedDB key types. The spec defines keys separately from values, and object stores are sorted by those keys. In practice, developers most often use numbers, strings, dates, or arrays of key-compatible values as keys. (
A realistic record looks like this:

{
  id: 1,
  name: "Luna",
  type: "Cat",
  age: 3,
  vaccinated: true,
  tags: ["friendly", "indoor"],
  photo: someBlob,
  adoptedAt: new Date()
}
That is the kind of structured object IndexedDB is designed to hold. (

Use a cursor when you want custom filtering, ordered traversal, or streaming through records one at a time.
is the standard iterator for walking an object store record by record. (

You can later retrieve pets by type:
This is one of the biggest reasons IndexedDB exists at all. Without indexes, you are just shoveling JSON into a browser cave. With indexes, you have searchable local data. (

People think IndexedDB works like SQL. It does not. There is no SQL query language built in; the spec explicitly describes a low-level API over records and indexes, and a query language could be layered on top, but is not built in. (
People try to read or write outside transactions. Also a bug factory. All data access is done within transactions. (
